 But — the Rule 15 question dominates and may change the whole shape. Rule 15 says all sequences are drafts; Steven reviews the Google Doc before anything ships. Auto-load-on-research-complete bypasses that
 review. If Rule 15 stands as-is, the real work isn't loader wiring — it's designing an approval trigger (e.g. a Telegram reply handler or /approve <slug> command) that fires execute_load_plan only after Steven
 signs off on the Doc. The loader wiring becomes a small piece of that larger approval flow.

 I want to resolve that before writing any plan. Two questions for you:

 1. Rule 15: Does "all sequences are drafts" still hold for diocesan/research-driven prospecting, or is there a class of sequences where auto-load-after-research is acceptable? If it holds universally, I'll
 plan the approval trigger instead of direct wiring.

 2. Contact source: Prep note open question 1 — does the research engine's result dict currently produce verified contact emails, or just district names? I'll need to read the actual research output shape in
 plan mode either way, but if you already know the answer it saves a step.

 Once you answer those, I'll Enter Plan Mode and work through the remaining prep-note questions (2, 3, 5, 6, 7) inside the plan. No code yet.

 research-complete time. The approval flow has to span create-sequence + contact-load, not just contact-load.

 ⏺ Found enough to answer Q2 myself. Reporting before plan mode.

 What I found (answering my own questions)

 Q1 (Rule 15): You confirmed — holds. All sequences stay drafts.

 Q2 (contact source): Research engine result dict DOES contain verified contacts. tools/research_engine.py:406 returns "contacts": self.all_contacts — a list of dicts with email , email_confidence ( "VERIFIED"
 etc.), and per-contact source metadata. So after a research job completes for a prospect, the machinery to populate execute_load_plan with real Contact records is already there. Good news.

 Q3 (I answered this one myself too, by reading agent/main.py:319-528 ): The current handler never calls outreach_client.create_sequence . It only writes a Google Doc via sequence_builder.write_sequence_to_doc
 and marks the queue row status="draft" with the Doc URL. There is no live Outreach sequence_id at draft time. That materially reframes the task.

 This reframes the wiring task

 sequence."

 So the real Session 64 build isn't one wiring. It's an approval-triggered two-step :

 1. /approve <name_key> flow (new): reads the saved draft from the queue row (or rebuilds from stored context), calls outreach_client.create_sequence with validator, returns a live Outreach sequence_id.

 2. Contact-load follow-up : takes the verified contacts from the research result (need to persist them somewhere between research-complete and approval — likely on the queue row or a sidecar file), calls
 prospect_loader.execute_load_plan with tightened sleep_seconds of (10, 30) seconds (sample, matching scripts/diocesan_drip.py ), wraps in run_in_executor per Rule 11.

 Both steps gated behind ENABLE_AUTO_LOAD_AFTER_APPROVAL = False kill switch per Rule 7.

 Open design decisions for plan mode:

 - Where do the research-time contacts get stored so /approve can find them later? The result dict isn't persisted today — needs a sidecar JSON per name_key, or a new column on the queue row.

 - What triggers /approve ? Telegram slash command? Reply-handler watching for "approved" / "good to go" on the draft-link message? You've told me natural language > slash commands
 ( feedback_natural_language_commands.md ), so leaning reply-handler.

 - How to handle drafts Steven gives edits on vs drafts he approves clean. The edit path needs to round-trip back through build_sequence with feedback baked in before the create_sequence step.

 - Where do the research-time contacts get stored so /approve can find them later? The result dict isn't persisted today — needs a sidecar JSON per name_key, or a new column on the queue row.
 at triggers /approve ? Telegram slash command? Reply-handler watching for "approv d" / "good o go" on the draf -link message? You've told me natural language > slash commands
 ( feedback_natural_language_commands.md ), s leaning reply-handler.
 - How to handle drafts Steven gives edits on vs drafts he approves clean. The edit path needs to round-trip back through build_sequence with feedback baked in before the create_sequence step.

 Ready to Enter Plan Mode on this reframed scope when you say go. Want me to proceed, or is there anything in this reframing you want to adjust first?

 ❯ ok as i read through this it makes me think -- havent we got these features built already? i already have had you draft me email sequences, i have sometimes gone to claude.ai to make the copy and then brought
 the finshed email sequences in here. (the interface is better in claude.ai for building nad editing email copy and subject lines) so i think id prefer that to be the workflow but iam open to whatever you
 think would be best. eventually we will have examples of all the different types of email templates and sequeneces for all the different scenarios, prospeting strategies, follow up, and every type of role and
 avatar - so at that point the changes to make new ones will be much simpler.

 But yes the flow would be understand what the email sequence is for and who we are targeting with it, draft up the copy and subject lines, I would go back and forth with claude.ai to dial in the copy, after
 that I would drop in the contacts/prospects/leads and we would neeed analyze tehm and then make segmented variation of the email sequences that are customized for the different roles/people (like admins
 teachers, IT, etc. ) after we have those different variations of the sequence we would bring those back to claude code along with the contact list and have claude generate the sequences in outreach.io
 properply - i would go verify everything looks right and activate them, then you woul add the prospects to those sequences but making sure they all have the state info and the time zones on them. And when
 doing a lot of contacts a single school district - make sure to stagger the sends for the prospects so it doesnt just look like spam.

 Short answer: yes, most of this is already built. You're describing the diocesan drip, generalized. The prep note I wrote was solving the wrong problem.

 Short answer: yes, most of this is already built. You're describing the diocesan drip, generalized. The prep note I wrote was solving the wrong problem.

 What already exists (in-repo, production-tested)

 - Draft generator + Google Doc round-trip: tools/sequence_builder.build_sequence + write_sequence_to_doc .

 - Outreach sequence create with full validator: tools/outreach_client.create_sequence — 12-check pre-write validator (sample: Session 59 round 4), post-write verify_sequence , owner/schedule/interval hardening,
 banned-phrase scrub.

 - Prospect create/dedup/add-to-sequence with timezone enforcement: tools/outreach_client.create_prospect + find_prospect_by_email + add_prospect_to_sequence . Rule 17 hard-required at validator boundary —
 missing IANA tz = contact skipped, never faked.

 - Bulk loader with stagger + day-bucket round-robin: tools/prospect_loader.build_load_plan + execute_load_plan . Already does resumable state, per-POST jitter sleep (tunable sleep_seconds ), and day-bucket
 round-robin so a single district's contacts spread across multiple days. This is exactly the "don't look like spam" stagger you described — already built.

 - End-to-end orchestrator for one vertical: scripts/diocesan_drip.py --execute . CSV in → Outreach load out, audit trail, Telegram heartbeat. This IS the workflow you described, hardcoded for the diocesan case.

 What's genuinely missing

 1. Role segmentation. Given a raw contact list, classify each contact by role (admin / teacher / IT / coach / etc.) and route to a role-specific sequence variant. Not built.

 2. Multi-variant orchestration. Given N role-variant copy sets, create N sequences in Outreach (one per variant) and load the right contact subset into each. Piece-by-piece exists; the loop around them does
 not.

 3. A strategy-agnostic drip runner. Generalize scripts/diocesan_drip.py into scripts/sequence_drip.py — takes (campaign_name, role→sequence_id map, contact CSV with state column), does the rest.

 4. A clean claude.ai → Claude Code paste format. A deterministic input shape (JSON or YAML frontmatter + markdown steps?) for finalized copy that create_sequence can consume without re-editing.

 What the prep note got wrong

 The _on_prospect_research_complete auto-load wiring is fighting your actual workflow. If claude.ai is the drafting surface, then auto-building drafts inside the async Railway handler is pointless — the handler
 should stop calling build_sequence at research-complete time, or at least stop writing the Doc, and just persist the research result (contacts + district intel) somewhere you can pick up in claude.ai. The S64
 prep note should be retired in favor of a new plan.

 Two confirmations before plan mode

 1. Kill the handler auto-draft path? Should _on_prospect_research_complete stop calling build_sequence / write_sequence_to_doc entirely and just persist the research result (contacts + intel) to a known
 location? Or keep the auto-draft as a starter-for-claude.ai? I lean toward killing it — less cruft, cleaner separation.

 2. Scope for Session 64 — full generalized drip + role segmentation + claude.ai paste format in one plan, or a first slice (e.g. generalized drip runner only, role segmentation later)? I lean toward slicing:
 ship the generalized drip runner first since the diocesan drip is 90% (sample — rough from reading) of the way there already, then role segmentation as its own plan.

 Once you answer those, I'll Enter Plan Mode on the right scope. ctx is 8% (measured — current marker), well under the 40% (measured — user CLAUDE.md) pause line, so plenty of runway for a proper plan this
 session.

 by role/avatar, stagger added later as a diocesan-specific refinement. That reframes the "what's missing" list — same machinery, but the use case is bigger batches + role variants from the start, not a weekly

 1. Why I proposed killing the handler auto-draft — and why I'm now walking it back

 - The handler at agent/main.py:319-528 auto-builds a draft sequence via sequence_builder.build_sequence the moment a research job finishes, writes it to a Google Doc, and sends you the link.

 - If your real drafting surface is claude.ai (better editing UX), then the in-handler draft becomes a throwaway starter — a sequence you'll rewrite anyway.

 - Every auto-draft is a Claude API call + Google Doc write + Telegram message. If it's thrown away, that's wasted cost and noise in your Telegram thread.

 - And the auto-draft lives inside the async Railway handler, which means every failure mode in build_sequence (Claude timeouts, voice profile loads, GAS bridge flakes) becomes a failure mode in the
 research-complete path.

 - The handler at agent/main.py:319-528 auto-builds a draft sequence via sequence_builder.build_sequence the moment a research job finishes, writes it to a Google Doc, and sends you the link.
 If your r al drafting surface is claude.ai (better editing UX), then the in-handler draft becomes a throwaway s arter — a sequence you'll rewrite anyway.
 Every auto-draft is a Claude API all + Googl Doc wr e + Telegram message. If it's thrown away, that's wasted cost and noise in your Telegram thread.
 And the auto-draft lives inside the async Railway handl r, which means every failure mode in build_sequence (Claude timeouts, voice p ofi loads, GAS bridge flakes) becomes a failure mode in the
 researc -complete path.

 Counterargument (stronger the more I think about it):

 - You said "I have sometimes gone to claude.ai to make the copy." Sometimes ≠ always. If the auto-draft is useful as a starter half the time and ignored the other half, killing it deletes a useful launchpad.

 - A starter draft anchored in the research result (actual district intel) might be higher-quality than starting from a blank claude.ai chat.

 - The cost is real but modest per call — not load-bearing unless the research engine is running hot.

 - You said "I have sometimes gone to claude.ai to make the copy." Sometimes ≠ always. If the auto-draft is useful as a starter half the time and ignored the other half, killing it deletes a useful launchpad.
 A starter draft anchored in th research result ( ctual district intel) might be higher-quali y th n starting from bl nk claude.ai chat.
 The cost is real but mo est per call — not load-bearing unless he r search engine is unning ho .

 The question I should actually be asking you: when the handler auto-draft fires after a research job, do you (a) use it as a starter and take it to claude.ai for iteration, (b) ignore it and draft from scratch
 in claude.ai, or (c) approve it as-is most of the time? Your answer changes the design. If (a) or (c), keep it. If (b), kill it.

 2. "All in one go" — what that means before I enter plan mode

 S64 plan covers:

 1. Generalized drip runner — scripts/sequence_drip.py . Takes a campaign config (name, role→sequence_id map, contact CSV with state column), does create_sequence + create_prospect + add_prospect_to_sequence
 with validator + timezone enforcement + same-district stagger.

 2. Role segmentation layer — tools/role_classifier.py . Given a contact (title, department, email), classify into admin / teacher / IT / curriculum / coach / other. Deterministic rule-based first pass, Claude
 fallback for ambiguous.

 3. Multi-variant campaign config format — the deterministic claude.ai → Claude Code paste format. Probably YAML frontmatter + markdown body per variant, one file per campaign in campaigns/<slug>/ . Schema
 validated at load time.

 4. Handler decision — kill the auto-draft, keep it, or refactor (depends on your answer to the question above).

 5. Kill switch — ENABLE_SEQUENCE_DRIP default OFF per Rule 7, commit-per-feature per Rule 8.

 1. Generalized drip runner — scripts/sequence_drip.py . Takes a campaign config (name, role→sequence_id map, contact CSV with state column), does create_sequence + create_prospect + add_prospect_to_sequence
 with validator + timezone enforcement + same-district st gger.
 2. Role segmentation layer — tools/role_classifier.py . Given a contact (title, department, email), classify into admin / teacher / IT / curriculum / coach / other. Deterministic rule-based first pass, Claude
 fallback for ambiguous.
 3. Multi-variant campaign config format — the deterministic claude.ai → Claude Code paste format. Probably YAML frontmatter + markdown body per variant, one file per campaign in campaigns/<slug>/ . Schema
 validated at load time.
 4. Handler decision — kill the auto-draft, keep it, or refactor (depends on your answer to the question above).
 5 Kill switch — ENABLE_SEQUENCE_DRIP default OFF per Rule 7, commit-per-feature per Rule 8.

 Honest estimate — this is a multi-commit session. Possibly 4 or 5 commits (rough — based on Rule 8 feature-per-commit and the 4 non-decision items above). ctx is 8% (measured — current marker) / 42% to EOS
 (measured — current marker) — so I can likely get through the plan + maybe the first one or two commits this session, remainder into next. I'll flag if that changes.

 Before I enter plan mode I need your answer to the handler question in (1) above. Once I have that, I'll Enter Plan Mode on the full scope.

 Ready to code?

 Here is Claude's plan:
 ╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌
 Plan — Generalized Campaign Drip Runner + Role Segmentation + Claude.ai Round-Trip

 Author: Claude (Session 64 plan mode)

 Supersedes: docs/session_64_prep_prospect_loader_wiring.md (which solved the wrong problem — see Context)

 ---

 Context

 Steven's actual campaign-load workflow is not what the S64 prep note assumed. The prep note assumed auto-load after research-complete, inside the async Railway handler. Steven's real workflow is:

 1. Understand the sequence purpose + audience (Steven's judgment).

 2. Draft copy + subject lines (either auto-draft from the handler as a starter, or an existing Outreach sequence as a starter).

 3. Iterate copy in claude.ai (better editing UX than Claude Code for prose work).

 4. Drop contacts into Claude Code.

 5. Segment contacts by role / avatar (admin vs teacher vs IT vs curriculum vs coach vs other).

 6. Make role-variant sequences — one copy set per role.

 7. Bring finalized variants back to Claude Code.

 8. Claude Code calls outreach_client.create_sequence per variant → N live sequences in Outreach.

 9. Steven manually verifies and activates each sequence in the Outreach UI (Rule 15: all sequences are drafts until Steven approves).

 10. Claude Code adds prospects to the right sequence per role, with timezone enforcement and same-district stagger.

 This pattern has been hand-coded three times in Scout history (S38 CUE conference loader, S43 C4 cold license request loader, S44 webinar tagging) before the S61 library promotion. The diocesan drip is the

 first committed, resumable, auditable instance. It is also already 90% strategy-agnostic — build_load_plan + execute_load_plan take pluggable callbacks for sequence routing, tagging, mailbox, days. What's

 missing is the orchestration layer on top and the role segmentation piece.

 The intended outcome: ship a strategy-agnostic campaign drip runner with deterministic role classification and a claude.ai round-trip paste format , so future CUE / C4 / webinar / conference / bond-trigger

 loads reuse library code instead of spawning new ephemeral scripts (the exact Rule 18 anti-pattern).

 Rule compliance notes:

 - Rule 1: plan-mode required (this file).

 - Rule 7: kill switches per new component.

 - Rule 8: one commit per feature (6 commits planned).

 - Rule 11: no sync time.sleep in async paths (the runner is a sync CLI script, so no issue).

 - Rule 15: ALL sequences are drafts until Steven activates in UI. Runner never activates; it only creates.

 - Rule 17: timezone enforcement already at validate_prospect_inputs — no new code needed.

 - Rule 18: promote ephemeral → library before any new use. This plan IS the promotion.

 - Rule 19: no Outreach numeric IDs in chat output; all CLI output translates IDs to human names.

 ---

 │ create_prospect / find_prospect_by_email / add_prospect_to_sequence │ tools/outreach_client.py │ 100% reusable. Called from inside execute_load_plan . │

 └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┴────────────────────────────────┴─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

 ---

 New components

 1. Campaign config schema ( tools/campaign_config.py — new)

 A @dataclass CampaignConfig plus a YAML loader.

 Purpose: single source of truth for a campaign's metadata — the "finished sequences + role variants + contact source" spec that sequence_drip.py consumes. Loaded from campaigns/<slug>/config.yaml (new

 directory, committed to repo).

 Directory convention:

 campaigns/

 cue_2026/

 config.yaml # campaign metadata + variant map

 variants/

 admin.md # finalized copy, one per role (frontmatter + body)

 teacher.md

 it.md

 curriculum.md

 coach.md

 contacts.csv # optional — inline contact source

 diocesan/

 config.yaml # refactored from scripts/diocesan_drip.py constants

 variants/

 superintendent.md # single variant (diocesan uses one role per diocese)

 config.yaml schema (minimal viable):

 campaign_name: "CUE 2026"

 campaign_slug: "cue_2026"

 mailbox_id: 11 # internal — never shown to Steven in CLI output

 owner_id: 11 # same

 schedule_id: 50 # C4 Tue-Thu (validator allowlist)

 drip_days: # ISO dates, round-robin across groups

 - 2026-04-21

 - 2026-04-22

 - 2026-04-23

 sleep_seconds_min: 10

 sleep_seconds_max: 30

 tag_template: "cue-2026-{group}"

 contact_source:

 type: "csv" # or "sheets" | "inline"

 path: "campaigns/cue_2026/contacts.csv"

 variants:

 admin:

 variant_file: "variants/admin.md"

 sequence_id: null # filled in after create_sequence runs

 teacher:

 variant_file: "variants/teacher.md"

 sequence_id: null

 # ...

 variants/<role>.md format:

 ---

 role: "admin"

 num _steps: 5

 target_ role _label: "CS Director"

 ---

 ## Step 1 — Subject: Quick note on your CS program

 Hi {{first_ name}},

 ...

 ## Step 2 — Subject: Re: Quick note on your CS program

 ...

 Loader parses frontmatter + markdown sections into a dict the sequence_builder + outreach_client.create_sequence chain can consume.

 Kill switch: none required (pure data loader).

 ---

 2. Role classifier ( tools/role_classifier.py — new)

 Function signature:

 def classify_contact_role(contact: dict ) -> str :

 """

 Classify a contact into one of: admin | teacher | it | curriculum | coach | other.

 Deterministic, rule-based, using agent/target_roles.py as the seed corpus.

 Args:

 contact: dict with at minimum "title" (str). Optional: "department",

 "email", "account", "notes".

 Returns:

 One of six role bucket strings. Never raises.

 """

 Bucket definitions (to be confirmed with Steven before implementation):

 ┌────────────┬────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────┐

 │ Bucket │ Title pattern examples │ Seed source │

 ├────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────┤

 │ admin │ Superintendent, Principal, Assistant Principal, Asst Supt, Executive Director, Head of School │ TIER1_TITLES subset │

 ├────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────┤

 │ curriculum │ Curriculum Director, C&I, Academic Coach, Instructional Coach, Assessment Coordinator │ TIER1_TITLES subset │

 ├────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────┤

 │ it │ Director of Technology, CIO, CTO, EdTech Coordinator, Instructional Technology (NOT IT_INFRA which is help desk / sysadmin — excluded) │ TECH_KEYWORDS ∩ EDUCATION_QUALIFIERS │

 ├────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────┤

 │ teacher │ CS Teacher, Math Teacher, Algebra Teacher, STEM Teacher, Robotics Teacher │ TIER2_TITLES subset │

 ├────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────┤

 │ coach │ Athletic Coach, Esports Coach, Robotics Coach, Game Design Coach │ TIER2_TITLES subset (coach-specific) │

 ├────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────┤

 │ other │ Falls through │ default │

 └────────────┴────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┴──────────────────────────────────────┘

 Pre-filter: call agent.target_roles.is_relevant_role(title) first. If it returns False (irrelevant CTE trades, IT_INFRA roles), return other directly and skip downstream routing.

 Classifier logic (v1 — rule-based only):

 1. Lowercase title.

 2. Strip punctuation.

 3. Test regex patterns per bucket in priority order (admin > curriculum > it > coach > teacher > other). First match wins.

 4. Fallback to other on no match.

 Deferred to v2 (not this plan): Claude-powered fallback for ambiguous titles. The classifier returns other conservatively in v1; Steven can eyeball + reclassify in the dry-run output before the real load runs.

 Kill switch: ROLE_CLASSIFIER_ENABLED = True constant at top of module. If False, every contact routes to other , which means every contact ends up in the default variant. Useful as an escape hatch.

 ---

 3. Generalized drip runner ( scripts/sequence_drip.py — new)

 Purpose: strategy-agnostic CLI that takes a CampaignConfig and does the full load orchestration. Diocesan drip becomes a thin wrapper around this (Commit 4).

 CLI interface:

 sequence_drip.py --campaign <slug> --assign # dry run: show plan

 sequence_drip.py --campaign <slug> --create # create_sequence for each variant

 sequence_drip.py --campaign <slug> --execute # load contacts (respects drip_days)

 sequence_drip.py --campaign <slug> --verify # post-load verification

 sequence_drip.py --campaign <slug> --dry-run # full plan, no Outreach writes

 Execution flow for --execute :

 1. Load campaigns/<slug>/config.yaml via campaign_config.load_campaign(slug) .

 2. Load contacts from contact_source (CSV / Sheets / inline).

 3. For each contact:

 - Derive timezone from state via state_to_timezone .

 - Skip contacts missing state or non-US (Rule 17 — never fall back).

 - Call role_classifier.classify_contact_role(contact) → role string.

 - Apply the matching variants[role].sequence_id as the target.

 4. Build list[Contact] with campaign_group=role for tag template substitution.

 5. Call build_load_plan(contacts, sequence_id_for=lambda c: cfg.variants[c.campaign_group].sequence_id, days=cfg.drip_days, mailbox_id=cfg.mailbox_id, tags_for=lambda c:

 [cfg.tag_template.format(group=c.campaign_group)]) .

 6. Call execute_load_plan(plans, state_path=f"data/{slug}_drip_state.json", audit_path=f"data/{slug}_drip_audit.jsonl", target_day=<today>, sleep_seconds=(cfg.sleep_seconds_min, cfg.sleep_seconds_max),

 verify_sequence_active=True) .

 7. Print a human-readable summary — NO raw Outreach IDs (Rule 19). Translate sequence_ids to variant role names, prospect_ids to "first last (email)".

 Execution flow for --create :

 1. Load campaign config.

 2. For each variant in config.variants :

 - If sequence_id already set (non-null), skip (idempotent).

 - Load variants/<role>.md , parse frontmatter + steps.

 - Build step dicts compatible with outreach_client.create_sequence signature.

 - Call create_sequence(name=f"{campaign_name} — {role}", steps=[...], schedule_id=cfg.schedule_id, ...) .

 - Write the returned sequence_id back to config.yaml under variants[role].sequence_id .

 3. Print a summary — "Created 5 variants for CUE 2026: admin, teacher, it, curriculum, coach. Verify + activate in Outreach, then run --execute."

 Kill switch: ENABLE_SEQUENCE_DRIP = True constant at top of file. Default True (this is a CLI, not an always-on scanner — the kill switch is more of a breakglass for catastrophic bugs than a production

 toggle).

 ---

 4. Diocesan drip wrapper refactor ( scripts/diocesan_drip.py — modify)

 Change: extract the diocesan-specific constants into campaigns/diocesan/config.yaml . The existing scripts/diocesan_drip.py stays in place as a backward-compatible wrapper that calls

 sequence_drip.run_campaign("diocesan", ...) with the same CLI flags Steven uses today ( --assign , --execute , --verify , --force-day , --canary , --canary-cleanup ).

 5. Outreach sequence export utility ( tools/outreach_client.py — add function)

 Function signature:

 def export_sequence_for_editing(sequence_id: int ) -> str :

 """

 Fetch an existing Outreach sequence and return it as markdown with

 YAML frontmatter — the same format as campaigns/<slug>/variants/<role>.md.

 Purpose: Steven pastes this into claude.ai as a starter for a new campaign.

 """

 Why: Steven said he "sometimes uses an old sequence or template as a starter." This utility closes the round-trip loop. Without it, the only way to get an existing Outreach sequence into claude.ai is to

 copy/paste manually from the Outreach UI.

 Implementation: calls existing get_sequence_steps(sequence_id) (already in outreach_client.py ), renders each step as a markdown section with subject + body, wraps in frontmatter (role, num_steps,

 target_role_label).

 Also add: a Telegram command hook in agent/main.py so Steven can type /export_sequence <id> and get the markdown back in chat. Scratch that — Rule 19 says no numeric IDs in chat. Better: /export_sequence

 <name> where name is a fuzzy match against sequence names, and internally it looks up the ID. Defer the Telegram hook to a follow-up session; ship the library function alone in this plan.

 a verified identical-output regression test. Safest: commit 5 ships Fri 2026-04-17 or later. This forces the split across sessions regardless of ctx pressure.

 You are acting as a ruthless, senior-level technical reviewer and architect.
 it, and hand back something I can execute with confidence. Do not hedge. Do not
 be diplomatic about problems. Be direct, specific, and opinionated.

 Before you begin: stop and think carefully about the entire plan as a whole.

 Don't rush into critique. Spend real effort understanding the full picture

 before you touch anything.

 1. Challenge the foundation. Before touching any details, ask whether the

 overall approach and architecture are right. Is this the best way to solve this

 problem, or did you default to the most obvious path? If there's a

 fundamentally better angle — a different architecture, a different sequencing,

 a different set of tradeoffs — say so and restructure around it. Don't anchor

 to your first answer just because you already wrote it.

 2. Stress-test every layer as if you're executing it yourself tomorrow. Walk

 through the plan step by step and ask: could I sit down and start building from

 this right now without getting stuck? Identify where assumptions are hiding,

 where steps are vague enough to stall momentum, where dependencies would create

 bottlenecks, and where things will quietly break at the edges. For every

 problem you find, name it, explain why it happens, explain what it costs, and

 provide the fix — not just the diagnosis.

 3. Validate against the best available knowledge. For each major technical

 decision — architecture, tooling, patterns, data modeling, sequencing —

 pressure-test whether your recommendation is genuinely the strongest option or

 just the most familiar. Think about what has worked well in production-grade

 projects, not just what's common in tutorials. If there are competing

 approaches with real tradeoffs, surface them explicitly and take a clear

 position on which I should use and why.

 4. Optimize for builder momentum. The plan should be structured so I can start

 executing immediately and maintain flow. Reorganize if needed so that early

 steps produce visible, tangible progress. Front-load decisions that unblock

 everything downstream. Flag anywhere the plan would force me to stop and make

 an ambiguous judgment call — then either make that call for me with your

 reasoning, or restructure so the decision isn't needed yet.

 5. Cut the fat, keep the muscle. Depth and complexity are welcome — but

 redundancy, vagueness, and ceremonial steps are not. Every element should earn

 its place. If something would make me pause and think "what does this actually

 mean in practice," either make it concrete or kill it. If two steps do the same

 work, collapse them.

 6. Fill the gaps a senior engineer would catch in a design review. What's

 missing that seems minor now but will cause real problems during execution?

 Think edge cases, error handling, sequencing issues, prerequisites, migration

 paths, and decisions that are easy to punt on but expensive to reverse later.

 7. Rewrite the full plan from scratch incorporating everything above. Do not

 patch the original — rebuild it with the benefit of everything you just

 identified. Make it comprehensive, precise, and immediately actionable. Then at

 the end, call out the most important changes you made and explain your

 reasoning so I can evaluate whether your thinking is sound.

 Take your time. Think deeply. Do your best work.

 What I found (answering my own questions)

 Q1 (Rule 15): You confirmed — holds. All sequences stay drafts.

 Q2 (contact source): Research engine result dict DOES contain verified contacts. tools/research_engine.py:406 returns "contacts": self.all_contacts — a list of dicts with email , email_confidence ( "VERIFIED"

 and marks the queue row status="draft" with the Doc URL. There is no live Outreach sequence_id at draft time. That materially reframes the task.

 sequence."

 So the real Session 64 build isn't one wiring. It's an approval-triggered two-step :

 1. /approve <name_key> flow (new): reads the saved draft from the queue row (or rebuilds from stored context), calls outreach_client.create_sequence with validator, returns a live Outreach sequence_id.

 2. Contact-load follow-up : takes the verified contacts from the research result (need to persist them somewhere between research-complete and approval — likely on the queue row or a sidecar file), calls

 prospect_loader.execute_load_plan with tightened sleep_seconds of (10, 30) seconds (sample, matching scripts/diocesan_drip.py ), wraps in run_in_executor per Rule 11.

 Both steps gated behind ENABLE_AUTO_LOAD_AFTER_APPROVAL = False kill switch per Rule 7.

 Short answer: yes, most of this is already built. You're describing the diocesan drip, generalized. The prep note I wrote was solving the wrong problem.

 What already exists (in-repo, production-tested)

 - Draft generator + Google Doc round-trip: tools/sequence_builder.build_sequence + write_sequence_to_doc .

 - Outreach sequence create with full validator: tools/outreach_client.create_sequence — 12-check pre-write validator (sample: Session 59 round 4), post-write verify_sequence , owner/schedule/interval hardening,

 banned-phrase scrub.

 - Prospect create/dedup/add-to-sequence with timezone enforcement: tools/outreach_client.create_prospect + find_prospect_by_email + add_prospect_to_sequence . Rule 17 hard-required at validator boundary —

 missing IANA tz = contact skipped, never faked.

 - Bulk loader with stagger + day-bucket round-robin: tools/prospect_loader.build_load_plan + execute_load_plan . Already does resumable state, per-POST jitter sleep (tunable sleep_seconds ), and day-bucket

 round-robin so a single district's contacts spread across multiple days. This is exactly the "don't look like spam" stagger you described — already built.

 - End-to-end orchestrator for one vertical: scripts/diocesan_drip.py --execute . CSV in → Outreach load out, audit trail, Telegram heartbeat. This IS the workflow you described, hardcoded for the diocesan case.

 What's genuinely missing

 1. Role segmentation. Given a raw contact list, classify each contact by role (admin / teacher / IT / coach / etc.) and route to a role-specific sequence variant. Not built.

 2. Multi-variant orchestration. Given N role-variant copy sets, create N sequences in Outreach (one per variant) and load the right contact subset into each. Piece-by-piece exists; the loop around them does

 not.

 3. A strategy-agnostic drip runner. Generalize scripts/diocesan_drip.py into scripts/sequence_drip.py — takes (campaign_name, role→sequence_id map, contact CSV with state column), does the rest.

 4. A clean claude.ai → Claude Code paste format. A deterministic input shape (JSON or YAML frontmatter + markdown steps?) for finalized copy that create_sequence can consume without re-editing.

 What the prep note got wrong

 The _on_prospect_research_complete auto-load wiring is fighting your actual workflow. If claude.ai is the drafting surface, then auto-building drafts inside the async Railway handler is pointless — the handler

 should stop calling build_sequence at research-complete time, or at least stop writing the Doc, and just persist the research result (contacts + district intel) somewhere you can pick up in claude.ai. The S64

 prep note should be retired in favor of a new plan.

 Two confirmations before plan mode

 1. Kill the handler auto-draft path? Should _on_prospect_research_complete stop calling build_sequence / write_sequence_to_doc entirely and just persist the research result (contacts + intel) to a known

 location? Or keep the auto-draft as a starter-for-claude.ai? I lean toward killing it — less cruft, cleaner separation.

 2. Scope for Session 64 — full generalized drip + role segmentation + claude.ai paste format in one plan, or a first slice (e.g. generalized drip runner only, role segmentation later)? I lean toward slicing:

 ship the generalized drip runner first since the diocesan drip is 90% (sample — rough from reading) of the way there already, then role segmentation as its own plan.

 Once you answer those, I'll Enter Plan Mode on the right scope. ctx is 8% (measured — current marker), well under the 40% (measured — user CLAUDE.md) pause line, so plenty of runway for a proper plan this

 session.

 ---

 by role/avatar, stagger added later as a diocesan-specific refinement. That reframes the "what's missing" list — same machinery, but the use case is bigger batches + role variants from the start, not a weekly

 drip.

 1. Why I proposed killing the handler auto-draft — and why I'm now walking it back

 - The handler at agent/main.py:319-528 auto-builds a draft sequence via sequence_builder.build_sequence the moment a research job finishes, writes it to a Google Doc, and sends you the link.

 - If your real drafting surface is claude.ai (better editing UX), then the in-handler draft becomes a throwaway starter — a sequence you'll rewrite anyway.

 - Every auto-draft is a Claude API call + Google Doc write + Telegram message. If it's thrown away, that's wasted cost and noise in your Telegram thread.

 - And the auto-draft lives inside the async Railway handler, which means every failure mode in build_sequence (Claude timeouts, voice profile loads, GAS bridge flakes) becomes a failure mode in the

 research-complete path.

 Counterargument (stronger the more I think about it):

 - You said "I have sometimes gone to claude.ai to make the copy." Sometimes ≠ always. If the auto-draft is useful as a starter half the time and ignored the other half, killing it deletes a useful launchpad.

 - A starter draft anchored in the research result (actual district intel) might be higher-quality than starting from a blank claude.ai chat.

 - The cost is real but modest per call — not load-bearing unless the research engine is running hot.

 The question I should actually be asking you: when the handler auto-draft fires after a research job, do you (a) use it as a starter and take it to claude.ai for iteration, (b) ignore it and draft from scratch

 in claude.ai, or (c) approve it as-is most of the time? Your answer changes the design. If (a) or (c), keep it. If (b), kill it.

 2. "All in one go" — what that means before I enter plan mode

 S64 plan covers:

 1. Generalized drip runner — scripts/sequence_drip.py . Takes a campaign config (name, role→sequence_id map, contact CSV with state column), does create_sequence + create_prospect + add_prospect_to_sequence

 with validator + timezone enforcement + same-district stagger.

 2. Role segmentation layer — tools/role_classifier.py . Given a contact (title, department, email), classify into admin / teacher / IT / curriculum / coach / other. Deterministic rule-based first pass, Claude

 fallback for ambiguous.

 3. Multi-variant campaign config format — the deterministic claude.ai → Claude Code paste format. Probably YAML frontmatter + markdown body per variant, one file per campaign in campaigns/<slug>/ . Schema

 validated at load time.

 4. Handler decision — kill the auto-draft, keep it, or refactor (depends on your answer to the question above).

 5. Kill switch — ENABLE_SEQUENCE_DRIP default OFF per Rule 7, commit-per-feature per Rule 8.

 Honest estimate — this is a multi-commit session. Possibly 4 or 5 commits (rough — based on Rule 8 feature-per-commit and the 4 non-decision items above). ctx is 8% (measured — current marker) / 42% to EOS

 (measured — current marker) — so I can likely get through the plan + maybe the first one or two commits this session, remainder into next. I'll flag if that changes.

 Before I enter plan mode I need your answer to the handler question in (1) above. Once I have that, I'll Enter Plan Mode on the full scope.

 ❯ i almost always use it as a starter, though sometimes i will tkae one onfmy old already created sequences or templates as a starter. we should defintiley keep it.

 ⏺ Decision locked: keep the handler auto-draft. Noting the "sometimes old sequence/template as starter" pattern — that adds a small piece to the scope: a utility to fetch an existing Outreach sequence and

 pretty-print it so you can feed it into claude.ai as a starter (call it outreach_client.export_sequence_for_editing or similar). Small add, fits the plan.

 Loading Enter Plan Mode and doing a bit more investigation before writing the plan.

 ⏺ Entered plan mode

 Claude is now exploring and designing an implementation approach.

 ⏺ Starting Phase 1: three parallel Explore agents to understand the existing machinery before designing.

 calls create_sequence ; the loader pattern has been hand-coded three times (CUE, C4, webinar) before the S61 library promotion.

 thought for 85s)

 ⏺ Rejecting v1 and rebuilding. Stopping to think through the whole plan as a system before touching anything.
 ---

 What I got wrong in v1 (the foundation)

 ⏸ plan mode on (shift+tab to cycle)

 What I got wrong in v1 (the foundation)

 1. Wrong schema shape for the claude.ai round-trip. I proposed campaigns/<slug>/config.yaml + variants/<role>.md + a YAML loader. That schema forces Steven to translate claude.ai's natural output (one
 conversational markdown blob) into a multi-file directory structure on every round trip. Friction at the worst point in the workflow. The correct shape is a single markdown file per campaign with frontmatter +
 ## variant: <role> sections, matching what claude.ai naturally produces. Copy-paste-save-run.

 ⏸ plan mode on (shift+tab to cycle)

 2. Wrong choice for the role classifier. I proposed a rule-based classifier using target_roles.py tier lists as seed data. Rule-based classifiers on job titles cap out at maybe 75-85% accuracy (estimate —
 ambiguous-title cases like "Director", "Coordinator", "Specialist" break deterministic rules) and require 150-300 lines of regex-plus-fallbacks to get there. Haiku at temp=0 will hit 92-97% (estimate) in 50
 lines of code for a per-contact cost under a penny (estimate — based on Haiku pricing and typical title length). Rule-based isn't "simpler" — it's a maintenance burden disguised as simplicity.

 ⏸ plan mode on (shift+tab to cycle)

 3. Including the diocesan refactor was a production-risk blunder. The Wed 2026-04-15 drip runs tomorrow and the Thu 2026-04-16 drip runs the day after (measured — from CLAUDE.md CURRENT STATE). Refactoring
 live production machinery mid-week, when the refactor is cosmetic and not required for any new campaign, is exactly the sort of thing that turns a clean plan into a Monday-morning incident. Kill it from the
 plan entirely. Diocesan drip stays untouched. The new loader lives alongside it. When diocesan next needs a change, that's when the migration happens.

 ⏸ plan mode on (shift+tab to cycle)

 4. Too many commits, overly ceremonial. Six commits with a schema-only commit, a separate docs commit, and a refactor commit that was already being cut. Collapse to four : parser + schema, role classifier,
 export utility, CLI orchestrator. Docs update rides on the last commit. No standalone "schema" commit — the parser test IS the schema validation.

 ⏸ plan mode on (shift+tab to cycle)

 5. Misnamed the script. sequence_drip.py implies time-spaced sends. Most campaigns are one-day bulk loads. The generalization is a campaign loader , not a drip. Rename to scripts/load_campaign.py .

 ⏸ plan mode on (shift+tab to cycle)

 6. Punted the hardest UX question. How does Steven get research-engine contacts INTO a campaign file? I handwaved "CSV". Reality: Steven is not going to CSV-export from Sheets for every campaign. The loader
 needs to accept stdin (paste) , path to a CSV , OR a Google Sheets tab reference . stdin is load-bearing — it's what makes the claude.ai round-trip work without an intermediate file.

 ⏸ plan mode on (shift+tab to cycle)

 7. Sidecar state mishandled. I wrote "store sequence_ids back into config.yaml" — which means mutating a source-of-truth file after --create runs, which is fragile and breaks git diffs. Correct pattern:
 sidecar state file at campaigns/<slug>.state.json (gitignored), separate from the campaign markdown.

 ⏸ plan mode on (shift+tab to cycle)

 8. Missing: pre-flight validator that surfaces ALL failures at once. outreach_client.validate_sequence_inputs returns failures one variant at a time. If Steven's campaign file has 5 variants and 4 have
 validator failures, he discovers them one-at-a-time across four --create runs. The loader needs to dry-run every variant through validate_sequence_inputs before any POST hits Outreach , and print all failures
 in one output. This is a required UX primitive, not a nice-to-have.

 ⏸ plan mode on (shift+tab to cycle)

 9. Missing: idempotency on --create . Running it twice shouldn't produce duplicate sequences. The sidecar state file solves this — skip variants whose sequence_id is already set.

 ⏸ plan mode on (shift+tab to cycle)

 [<u[>1u[>4;2m

 10. Missing: what happens when Steven edits copy mid-campaign. After --create runs, the sequence is live in Outreach. Editing campaigns/cue_2026.md doesn't update Outreach. v1 needs to warn on this (compare
 state-file snapshot hash to current file hash) and leave --update as a v2 feature.

 ⏸ plan mode on (shift+tab to cycle)

 ⏸ plan mode on (shift+tab to cycle)

 Ready to code?

 Here is Claude's plan:
 ╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌
 Plan — Generalized Campaign Loader with Role Segmentation and Claude.ai Round-Trip

 Rev 2 — rebuilt from scratch after v1 pressure-test (2026-04-14 S64 plan mode)

 ---

 Context

 Steven's production workflow for campaign loads is:

 1. Draft copy and subjects (starter comes from either the handler auto-draft at agent/main.py:482 or an existing Outreach sequence copied out as a starter).

 2. Iterate the copy in claude.ai — that's the preferred drafting surface because the editing UX beats Claude Code for prose work.

 3. Drop contacts into Claude Code.

 4. Classify each contact by role/avatar (admin / curriculum / IT / teacher / coach / other).

 5. Produce role-variant copy sets (one per bucket).

 6. Call outreach_client.create_sequence per variant to create real live sequences in Outreach.

 7. Steven verifies and activates each sequence manually in the Outreach UI (Rule 15 — all sequences are drafts until Steven signs off).

 8. Claude Code loads the right contacts into the right sequences, with IANA timezone enforcement and same-district stagger (via execute_load_plan sleep_seconds, already built).

 This pattern has been hand-coded three times in Scout history: S38 CUE conference loader ( scripts/enrich_cue_leads.py , 2,422 lines, commit 7d9a539 ), S43 C4 cold license request loader

 ( scripts/create_c4_sequences.py + enrich_c4_pass2.py , 1,622 lines combined, commit 353b1f0 ), and S44 webinar registrant tagging ( aabdb0c ). The S61 library promotion ( fdf6920 ) built create_prospect /

 find_prospect_by_email / add_prospect_to_sequence / build_load_plan / execute_load_plan but stopped short of the orchestration layer — the diocesan drip is the only committed campaign runner, and it's

 diocesan-specific at the CLI boundary (6 hardcoded sequence IDs 2008-2013, hardcoded DRIP_DAYS, hardcoded MAILBOX_ID).

 Intended outcome: ship a strategy-agnostic campaign loader so the next CUE / C4 / webinar / bond-trigger push uses library code instead of spawning a 4th ephemeral script. Rule 18 is the driving constraint —

 "promote ephemeral patterns before using them."

 Rule compliance:

 - Rule 1 — plan-mode required (this file).

 - Rule 7 — kill switches per component.

 - Rule 8 — one commit per feature.

 - Rule 11 — loader is a sync CLI script, not async; no time.sleep /async conflict.

 - Rule 15 — loader creates sequences but never activates; Steven activates manually in Outreach UI.

 - Rule 17 — timezone enforcement already handled at validate_prospect_inputs boundary.

 - Rule 18 — this plan IS the promotion; the promoted pattern is a single entry point ( scripts/load_campaign.py ) that future work reuses.

 - Rule 19 — loader output translates all Outreach IDs to human names before printing; no numeric IDs in stdout.

 ---

 Architectural decisions (locked)

 Decision 1 — Single markdown file per campaign, not a directory

 A campaign lives at campaigns/<slug>.md — one file, YAML frontmatter for metadata, ## variant: <role> H2 sections for each role's copy.

 Why: claude.ai produces conversational markdown. Directory-per-campaign with config.yaml + variants/*.md forces Steven to translate claude.ai output into a new schema on every round trip. Single-file lets him

 copy-paste-save.

 Schema (concrete, committed):

 ---

 campaign _name: "CUE 2026"

 campaign_ slug: "cue _2026"

 schedule_ id: 50 # C4 Tue-Thu

 drip _days:

 - 2026-04-21

 - 2026-04-22

 - 2026-04-23

 tag_ template: "cue-2026-{role}"

 sleep _seconds_ min: 60 # per-POST stagger, seconds

 sleep _seconds_ max: 180

 ---

 ## variant: admin

 target _role_ label: "Superintendent / Principal"

 num _steps: 5

 ### Step 1 — Subject: Quick note on your CS rollout at {{company}}

 Hi {{first_ name}},

 ...

 ### Step 2 — Subject: Re: Quick note on your CS rollout at {{company}}

 ...

 ## variant: teacher

 target _role_ label: "CS Teacher / Math Teacher"

 num _steps: 5

 ### Step 1 — Subject: ...

 ...

 What's NOT in the frontmatter: mailbox_id and owner_id . Both are hardcoded to 11 in outreach_client (Steven is the only user). Putting them in the schema adds surface area Steven will forget to set.

 Parser contract ( tools/campaign_file.py ):

 @dataclass

 class CampaignStep:

 subject: str

 body_html: str # parsed from the markdown body — plain text acceptable

 interval_seconds: int # default from a constant, not in schema

 @dataclass

 class CampaignVariant:

 role: str # "admin" | "curriculum" | "it" | "teacher" | "coach" | "other"

 target_role_label: str

 num_steps: int

 steps: list [CampaignStep]

 @dataclass

 class Campaign:

 name: str

 slug: str

 schedule_id: int

 drip_days: list [date]

 tag_template: str

 sleep_seconds: tuple [ int , int ]

 variants: dict [ str , CampaignVariant]

 def load_campaign(path: Path) -> Campaign:

 """Parse campaigns/<slug>.md into a Campaign. Permissive markdown parser."""

 def dump_campaign(campaign: Campaign) -> str :

 """Inverse — for export_sequence_for_editing round-trip."""

 Decision 2 — Claude Haiku for role classification, not rule-based

 tools/role_classifier.py calls Haiku at temp=0 with a six-bucket prompt per contact, results cached by SHA1 of the normalized title to avoid re-calling on reruns.

 Why: rule-based on job titles caps at 75-85% (estimate — ambiguous-title failure modes like "Director" / "Coordinator" / "Specialist"). Haiku at temp=0 should hit 92-97% (estimate). Cost estimate: under $0.002

 per contact (estimate — Haiku input ~400 tokens, output ~20 tokens, at Haiku 4.5 pricing). A 1,119-contact C4 batch costs under $3 (estimate) — well inside the $25/week research budget.

 Interface:

 def classify_contact_role(contact: dict , *, cache_path: Path | None = None ) -> str :

 """

 Return one of: admin, curriculum, it, teacher, coach, other.

 Never raises — unclassifiable contacts return 'other'.

 """

 Pre-filter: call agent.target_roles.is_relevant_role(title) first. If False → return other without calling Claude. Saves cost on trades / IT_INFRA rows.

 Bucket definitions (in the Haiku prompt, not in code):

 ┌────────────┬────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐

 │ Bucket │ Includes │

 ├────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤

 │ admin │ Superintendents, Principals, Assistant Principals, Asst Supts, Executive Directors, Heads of School, District Office senior leadership │

 ├────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤

 │ curriculum │ Curriculum Directors, C&I, Academic Coaches, Instructional Coaches, Assessment Coordinators, CS Curriculum Leads │

 ├────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤

 │ it │ Directors of Technology, CIOs, CTOs, EdTech Coordinators, Instructional Technology Coordinators (explicitly NOT help desk / sysadmin — those fall through to other via is_relevant_role │

 │ │ pre-filter) │

 ├────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤

 │ teacher │ CS Teachers, Math Teachers, Algebra Teachers, STEM Teachers, Robotics Teachers, classroom-level roles │

 ├────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤

 │ coach │ Athletic Coaches, Esports Coaches, Robotics Coaches, Game Design Coaches, anything explicitly "coach" in title that isn't academic coaching │

 ├────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤

 │ other │ Anything that doesn't fit cleanly │

 └────────────┴────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

 Kill switch: ROLE_CLASSIFIER_MODE = "haiku" at top of file. Set to "pass_through" to route every contact to other — useful as a breakglass if Haiku is down.

 Decision 3 — Scrap the diocesan refactor

 The Wed 2026-04-15 drip runs tomorrow and the Thu 2026-04-16 drip runs the day after. Refactoring the live production diocesan drip mid-week carries real incident risk and zero reward — the generalized loader

 doesn't NEED to share a code path with diocesan drip to ship. scripts/diocesan_drip.py stays exactly as-is. Future migration (if it happens) is a separate plan, scheduled well after both drip runs complete and

 only when a genuine reason to converge the two paths appears.

 Decision 4 — Sidecar state, not in-file mutation

 After --create runs, sequence IDs are written to campaigns/<slug>.state.json (gitignored). The markdown file stays clean and git-diffable.

 State file schema:

 {

 "slug" : "cue_2026" ,

 "created_at" : "2026-04-14T13:45:00-05:00" ,

 "campaign_file_sha1" : "abc123..." ,

 "sequences" : {

 "admin" : { "id" : 2014 , "name" : "CUE 2026 — admin" , "created_at" : "..." },

 "teacher" : { "id" : 2015 , "name" : "CUE 2026 — teacher" , "created_at" : "..." }

 },

 "load_runs" : [

 { "started_at" : "..." , "day" : "2026-04-21" , "contacts_loaded" : 14 , "errors" : 0 }

 ]

 }

 Idempotency: --create reads sequences dict first; any variant already present (non-null id) is skipped. Re-running --create is safe.

 Drift detection: --execute compares campaign_file_sha1 against the current file. Mismatch → warn loudly ("you edited the campaign file after --create; sequences in Outreach no longer match") and require

 --force to proceed.

 Decision 5 — Three contact input modes

 The loader accepts contacts from:

 1. --contacts-csv <path> — CSV at that path. Columns: first_name, last_name, email, title, company, state, notes (minimum). Extra columns passed through to the classifier.

 2. --contacts-stdin — same CSV format piped on stdin. Enables: Steven pastes a CSV blob in Claude Code, I pipe it through the command with a heredoc or file substitution.

 3. --contacts-sheet <tab_name> — Google Sheets tab reference (uses existing GAS bridge). Reuses the diocesan drip's _lead_to_contact adapter pattern, genericized.

 v1 scope: ship CSV + stdin. Sheets adapter deferred to v2 (it's a nice-to-have, not load-bearing for the claude.ai round-trip flow).

 Decision 6 — Preflight validator, loud-and-early

 Before any --create POSTs fire, load_campaign.py runs every variant through outreach_client.validate_sequence_inputs in a dry-run pass and prints all failures at once. No partial creates. If any variant fails,

 exit non-zero and print the full failure table.

 Same for --execute : before loading contacts, check that (a) all target sequences in the state file are enabled in Outreach, (b) every contact has a valid IANA timezone via state_to_timezone , (c) the contact

 list has no obvious issues (empty email, missing state). Any failure → exit non-zero with the full failure list.

 Decision 7 — Export utility as its own tool, not a Telegram command

 outreach_client.export_sequence_for_editing(sequence_id) -> str returns a markdown blob in the same schema as Decision 1. Steven pipes the output to a new campaigns/<slug>.md file, edits in claude.ai, saves.

 ---

 ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐

 │ Piece │ Location │ Role in plan │

 ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤

 │ Contact dataclass │ tools/prospect_loader.py:56-74 │ Target shape for CSV/stdin → loader boundary. diocese_or_group field = generic campaign_group label │

 │ │ │ (no rename). │

 ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤

 │ build_load_plan(...) │ tools/prospect_loader.py:117 │ Called from load_campaign --execute . Callbacks: sequence_id_for routes by classified role, tags_for │

 │ │ │ applies tag_template , days = campaign drip_days . │

 ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤

 │ execute_load_plan(...) │ tools/prospect_loader.py:259 │ Called from load_campaign --execute . State path = campaigns/<slug>.state.json , audit path = │

 │ │ │ campaigns/<slug>.audit.jsonl . │

 ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤

 │ create_sequence(...) │ tools/outreach_client.py │ Called from load_campaign --create . Validator auto-runs. │

 ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤

 │ validate_sequence_inputs(...) │ tools/outreach_client.py:814 │ Called manually in the --create preflight pass to surface all failures before any POST. │

 ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤

 │ get_sequence_steps(...) │ tools/outreach_client.py │ Called from export_sequence_for_editing to pull an existing sequence out. │

 ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤

 │ create_prospect / find_prospect_by_email / add_prospect_to_sequence │ tools/outreach_client.py │ All called from inside execute_load_plan . Zero new wiring. │

 │ / validate_prospect_inputs │ │ │

 ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤

 │ state_to_timezone(state) │ tools/timezone_lookup.py │ Called in contact hydration; skip contacts with missing or non-US state. │

 ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤

 │ is_relevant_role(title) │ agent/target_roles.py:333 │ Pre-filter before Haiku classification. │

 └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┴────────────────────────────────┴─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

 ---

 New files:

 - tools/campaign_file.py — Campaign dataclass + markdown parser + dumper

 - tools/role_classifier.py — Haiku-backed role bucketing

 - scripts/load_campaign.py — CLI orchestrator

 - scripts/test_campaign_file.py — parser round-trip + edge-case tests

 - scripts/test_role_classifier.py — fixture-based classifier tests (uses a small mock Claude client for determinism)

 - campaigns/canary_test.md — canary fixture (3 fake variants, 5 fake contacts inline in a ## contacts section)

 - .gitignore append — campaigns/*.state.json , campaigns/*.audit.jsonl

 - tools/outreach_client.py — add export_sequence_for_editing(sequence_id) -> str

 - docs/SCOUT_CAPABILITIES.md — document new components

 - CLAUDE.md — add a "PREFLIGHT: Campaign load" checklist + update CURRENT STATE

 Files NOT modified: agent/main.py , scripts/diocesan_drip.py , tools/prospect_loader.py , tools/sequence_builder.py , agent/target_roles.py .

 ---

 Commit plan (four commits, stop-anywhere safe)

 Commit 1 — feat(campaign_file): single-file campaign markdown schema + parser

 - New: tools/campaign_file.py , scripts/test_campaign_file.py , campaigns/canary_test.md

 - Tests:

 - Round-trip — parse canary → dump → parse → equality.

 - Permissive parser — handles ### Step N — Subject: with variations in heading level ( ## , ### , #### ) and subject separator ( — , - , : ).

 - Missing frontmatter key → clear error with key name.

 - Duplicate variant roles → clear error.

 - Empty variant section → treated as no variant.

 - Verification: .venv/bin/python scripts/test_campaign_file.py must pass.

 - Stop-anywhere: this commit ships a usable parser even if nothing else ships. Unit-tested in isolation.

 Commit 2 — feat(outreach): export_sequence_for_editing — fetch sequence as claude.ai starter

 - Modified: tools/outreach_client.py — new export_sequence_for_editing(sequence_id: int) -> str . Calls get_sequence_steps , renders markdown matching Commit 1 schema, wraps in frontmatter.

 - Smoke test: export diocesan Chicago sequence (sequence "Chicago diocesan" per Rule 19 name translation) → print to stdout → eyeball output → round-trip through campaign_file.load_campaign parser (should

 parse cleanly as a one-variant campaign).

 - Verification: .venv/bin/python -c "from tools.outreach_client import Outreach Client; print(Outreach Client().export_sequence_for_editing(2013))" writes a well-formed markdown blob.

 - Stop-anywhere: shippable even without the classifier or CLI. Steven can use it manually from a Python shell.

 Commit 3 — feat(role_classifier): Haiku-backed contact role bucketing with cache

 - New: tools/role_classifier.py , scripts/test_role_classifier.py

 - Tests: fixtures for each of the 6 buckets plus 5 ambiguous edge cases ( "Director" , "Coordinator" , "Specialist" , "Lead" , "Administrator" — ambiguous alone, needs Haiku). Uses a mock Claude client for

 deterministic testing.

 - Cache: JSON at data/role_classifier_cache.json . Key = sha1(normalized title), value = bucket string + timestamp.

 - Verification:

 a. .venv/bin/python scripts/test_role_classifier.py must pass.

 b. Manual smoke: classify 10 real titles from memory/csta_roster.json (existing CSTA roster data) and eyeball the output. Accept if 9 of 10 are correct.

 - Stop-anywhere: shippable even without the CLI. Can be called from a Python shell for ad-hoc classification.

 Commit 4 — feat(load_campaign): generalized campaign loader CLI + canary smoke test + docs

 - New: scripts/load_campaign.py

 - Modified: docs/SCOUT_CAPABILITIES.md , CLAUDE.md (new PREFLIGHT: Campaign load checklist + CURRENT STATE update)

 - CLI flags:

 load_campaign.py --campaign <slug> --create # preflight + create sequences

 load_campaign.py --campaign <slug> --preview # dry-run of --execute plan

 load_campaign.py --campaign <slug> --execute # load contacts (respects drip_days)

 load_campaign.py --campaign <slug> --execute --force # bypass drift check

 load_campaign.py --contacts-csv <path> [...] # CSV contact source

 load_campaign.py --contacts-stdin [...] # piped CSV on stdin

 - Verification:

 a. .venv/bin/python scripts/load_campaign.py --campaign canary_test --preview → prints plan with 5 contacts classified into 3 buckets, routed to placeholder sequence IDs, no API calls.

 b. Integration test (manual, not automated): Steven runs --create --preview against canary_test, inspects the validator output, no writes fire.

 - Stop-anywhere: this is the terminal commit. Session 64 ends cleanly whether or not this commit lands.

 ---

 Preflight additions to CLAUDE.md (Commit 4)

 PREFLIGHT: Campaign load — triggers on any task running load_campaign.py or creating/editing files under campaigns/ .

 - Confirm the campaign markdown file parses cleanly via tools.campaign_file.load_campaign(path) — any parse error = STOP.

 - Confirm the schedule_id is in the validator allowlist (env OUTREACH_ALLOWED_SCHEDULE_IDS default {48, 50, 51, 52, 53} ).

 - Confirm every variant has a meeting link hyperlink in at least one step and codecombat.com/schools hyperlink in at least two — matches feedback_sequence_copy_rules.md .

 - Confirm drip_days are all business days, in the future, and in a reasonable range (not > 30 days out).

 - Run --create --preview first (dry-run pass through validate_sequence_inputs ); fix all failures BEFORE running --create for real.

 - After --create , open Outreach UI and activate each new sequence. --execute will refuse to load contacts into disabled sequences ( verify_sequence_active=True is the default).

 - Before --execute , confirm every contact has a populated state column that maps via state_to_timezone to a non-empty IANA tz. Missing = skip contact, never fall back (Rule 17).

 - Check sending cap headroom — the 5k/rolling-7-days per-user Outreach cap ( feedback_outreach_sending_cap_5k_weekly.md ).

 - Never bypass validate_sequence_inputs ; never pass verify_sequence_active=False without explicit reason.

 ---

 Verification plan

 Unit level (all automated, run in CI-style via .venv/bin/python ):

 .venv/bin/python scripts/test_campaign_file.py

 .venv/bin/python scripts/test_role_classifier.py

 Both must exit 0 before any commit.

 Integration level (manual smoke tests against canary):

 # Parses the canary fixture, classifies 5 fake contacts, prints the plan:

 .venv/bin/python scripts/load_campaign.py --campaign canary_test --preview

 # Runs the --create preflight (validator pass) against canary variants, no writes:

 .venv/bin/python scripts/load_campaign.py --campaign canary_test --create --preview

 Export utility smoke test:

 # Exports the Chicago diocesan sequence as a markdown starter:

 .venv/bin/python -c "from tools.outreach_client import Outreach Client; print(Outreach Client().export_sequence_for_editing(2013))" > /tmp/chicago_export.md

 .venv/bin/python -c "from tools.campaign_file import load_campaign; print(load_campaign('/tmp/chicago_export.md'))"

 Both must run without error and the parsed Campaign object must contain one variant with 5 steps.

 Live test (deferred to a later session, gated on Steven's approval):

 Real campaign — next CUE conference push or next C4 re-engagement batch. Run --create , Steven activates in Outreach UI, run --execute , verify contacts land in the right sequences via the Outreach UI. This is

 NOT a Session 64 deliverable.

 ---

 Out of scope (explicitly deferred)

 1. Diocesan drip refactor. Not until a genuine reason to converge the two paths appears, and never mid-week during a live drip.

 2. Google Sheets contact source adapter. CSV + stdin only for v1.

 3. --update command to PATCH existing sequences after copy edits. v1 warns on drift; v2 (if needed) adds PATCH.

 4. Telegram /export_sequence <name> hook. Library function ships; Telegram hook waits.

 5. Claude-powered campaign DRAFTING (vs classification). Drafting stays in claude.ai per Steven's workflow.

 6. Contact source from research engine result dict directly. The research engine writes to Sheets; the loader reads from CSV/stdin. Bridging these directly is a post-v1 convenience feature.

 7. Automated role classifier accuracy measurement. v1 eyeballs 10 real titles and accepts if 9 pass. Formal eval suite is v2.

 ---

 Session-crossover strategy

 ctx is 9% (measured — current injected marker) as of plan write. Each commit is independently shippable. Recommended execution order for Session 64:

 1. Commit 1 (campaign parser) — ~45 min (estimate). Pure data, no external deps. Safe to ship even late in the session.

 2. Commit 2 ( export_sequence_for_editing ) — ~45 min (estimate). One Outreach API call plus markdown rendering. Testable in isolation.

 3. Commit 3 (role classifier) — ~60 min (estimate). Includes Haiku smoke test against CSTA roster titles.

 4. Commit 4 (CLI + docs) — ~75-90 min (estimate). The orchestrator tying everything together. If ctx is tight, split this across Session 64 / Session 65.

 Any commit boundary is a clean resume point. The CLI in Commit 4 depends on 1+2+3; everything else is independent.

 ---

 Most important changes from v1 (changelog)

 1. Killed the diocesan refactor entirely. v1 put it in Commit 5, which would have forced production-risk changes on the day before the Wed 2026-04-15 drip and the Thu 2026-04-16 drip. No upside, real downside.

 Commit count dropped from 6 to 4.

 2. Single markdown file per campaign replaces config.yaml + variants/ directory. The directory layout forced Steven to translate claude.ai output into a new schema on every round trip. Single-file matches

 claude.ai's natural output — copy-paste-save-run. The parser becomes slightly more complex (one parser vs one YAML lib call plus one markdown lib call), but the UX win is the whole point.

 3. Role classifier is Haiku-backed, not rule-based. Rule-based on job titles hits 75-85% (estimate) and needs 150-300 lines of regex-plus-fallbacks. Haiku at temp=0 hits 92-97% (estimate) in ~50 lines at under

 $0.002/contact (estimate). v1's "simpler = rule-based" instinct was wrong — Haiku is genuinely simpler once you stop pretending title parsing is solvable deterministically.

 4. Sidecar state file for sequence_ids , not in-file mutation. v1 proposed writing sequence_ids back to config.yaml , which would dirty the markdown file after every --create run and pollute git diffs. The

 sidecar is gitignored, tracks drift via campaign file sha1, and enables clean idempotent re-runs of --create .

 5. Three contact input modes (CSV + stdin + Sheets), v1 scope is CSV + stdin. v1 said "CSV only" and ignored that Steven won't CSV-export from Sheets for every campaign. stdin is load-bearing — it's what makes

 the claude.ai round-trip work without an intermediate file.

 6. Preflight validator runs all variants BEFORE any POST. v1 didn't design this. Without it, Steven discovers validator failures one-at-a-time across multiple --create runs (because each failure aborts the

 next variant's creation). The new preflight is a single dry-run pass through validate_sequence_inputs that prints all failures at once.

 7. Drift detection via campaign file sha1. v1 had no guard against Steven editing the campaign file after --create ran. The state file stores the sha1 of the markdown file at --create time; --execute compares

 and warns loudly on mismatch. --force overrides for edge cases.

 8. Renamed sequence_drip.py → load_campaign.py . "Drip" implies time-spaced sends. Most campaigns are one-day bulk loads. The generalization is a campaign loader, not a drip.

 9. Bucketed classifier deferred to v2: Claude-powered ambiguity fallback, role reclassification UI, and accuracy eval suite. v1 is the minimum viable classifier — Haiku with a prompt and a cache.

 10. Dropped tools/campaign_config.py from the plan. v1 had it as a separate module; it's now merged into tools/campaign_file.py as a single parser-plus-dataclass module. One module, one responsibility (parsing

 the campaign markdown file into typed data).

 ---

 Critical files to read before starting implementation

 1. tools/prospect_loader.py:56-74 — Contact dataclass contract (field names used by build_load_plan ).

 2. tools/prospect_loader.py:117 — build_load_plan signature ( sequence_id_for callback shape, tags_for callback shape, days type).

 3. tools/prospect_loader.py:259 — execute_load_plan signature ( state_path , audit_path , sleep_seconds tuple format).

 4. tools/outreach_client.py:814 — validate_sequence_inputs return shape ( {passed, failures, warnings} ).

 5. tools/outreach_client.py — get_sequence_steps signature (what shape the steps come back in, for export_sequence_for_editing ).

 6. tools/outreach_client.py — create_sequence signature (what fields are required, what the step dict should look like).

 7. agent/target_roles.py:333 — is_relevant_role(title) exact behavior (for the classifier pre-filter).

 8. scripts/diocesan_drip.py:116-142 — _lead_to_contact adapter pattern (reference for the CSV → Contact conversion, not for copying).

 9. agent/claude_brain.py — Anthropic client instantiation pattern (what client the classifier should import or instantiate).

 Short answer: yes, most of this is already built. You're describing the diocesan drip, generalized. The prep note I wrote was solving the wrong problem.

 What already exists (in-repo, production-tested)

 - Draft generator + Google Doc round-trip: tools/sequence_builder.build_sequence + write_sequence_to_doc .

 - Outreach sequence create with full validator: tools/outreach_client.create_sequence — 12-check pre-write validator (sample: Session 59 round 4), post-write verify_sequence , owner/schedule/interval hardening,

 banned-phrase scrub.

 - Prospect create/dedup/add-to-sequence with timezone enforcement: tools/outreach_client.create_prospect + find_prospect_by_email + add_prospect_to_sequence . Rule 17 hard-required at validator boundary —

 missing IANA tz = contact skipped, never faked.

 - Bulk loader with stagger + day-bucket round-robin: tools/prospect_loader.build_load_plan + execute_load_plan . Already does resumable state, per-POST jitter sleep (tunable sleep_seconds ), and day-bucket

 round-robin so a single district's contacts spread across multiple days. This is exactly the "don't look like spam" stagger you described — already built.

 - End-to-end orchestrator for one vertical: scripts/diocesan_drip.py --execute . CSV in → Outreach load out, audit trail, Telegram heartbeat. This IS the workflow you described, hardcoded for the diocesan case.

 What's genuinely missing

 1. Role segmentation. Given a raw contact list, classify each contact by role (admin / teacher / IT / coach / etc.) and route to a role-specific sequence variant. Not built.

 2. Multi-variant orchestration. Given N role-variant copy sets, create N sequences in Outreach (one per variant) and load the right contact subset into each. Piece-by-piece exists; the loop around them does

 not.

 3. A strategy-agnostic drip runner. Generalize scripts/diocesan_drip.py into scripts/sequence_drip.py — takes (campaign_name, role→sequence_id map, contact CSV with state column), does the rest.

 4. A clean claude.ai → Claude Code paste format. A deterministic input shape (JSON or YAML frontmatter + markdown steps?) for finalized copy that create_sequence can consume without re-editing.

 What the prep note got wrong

 The _on_prospect_research_complete auto-load wiring is fighting your actual workflow. If claude.ai is the drafting surface, then auto-building drafts inside the async Railway handler is pointless — the handler

 should stop calling build_sequence at research-complete time, or at least stop writing the Doc, and just persist the research result (contacts + district intel) somewhere you can pick up in claude.ai. The S64

 prep note should be retired in favor of a new plan.

 Two confirmations before plan mode

 1. Kill the handler auto-draft path? Should _on_prospect_research_complete stop calling build_sequence / write_sequence_to_doc entirely and just persist the research result (contacts + intel) to a known

 location? Or keep the auto-draft as a starter-for-claude.ai? I lean toward killing it — less cruft, cleaner separation.

 2. Scope for Session 64 — full generalized drip + role segmentation + claude.ai paste format in one plan, or a first slice (e.g. generalized drip runner only, role segmentation later)? I lean toward slicing:

 ship the generalized drip runner first since the diocesan drip is 90% (sample — rough from reading) of the way there already, then role segmentation as its own plan.

 Once you answer those, I'll Enter Plan Mode on the right scope. ctx is 8% (measured — current marker), well under the 40% (measured — user CLAUDE.md) pause line, so plenty of runway for a proper plan this

 session.
